sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 133 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.4x over the target.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing UP twice:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18 -> rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (moved up 10 rows)
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43 -> rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (moved up 10 rows)
Wait, the right feet moved from rows 54-58 to rows 44-48, which is 10 rows up. But the left feet moved from rows 19-23 to rows 14-18, which is only 5 rows up. Wait, that's wrong. Let me recalculate.
Initial state before UP, UP: rows 19-23, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (right)
After first UP: rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 49-53, cols 39-43 (right)
After second UP: rows 9-13, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (right)
Wait, but the output says rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (right). So the left feet only moved up by 5 rows (from rows 19-23 to rows 14-18), not 10 rows.
Hmm, that's strange. Let me check if the left feet were blocked from moving up the second time.
Actually, looking at the output, the left feet are at rows 14-18, which is 5 rows up from rows 19-23. So the first UP moved the left feet from rows 19-23 to rows 14-18, and the second UP didn't move the left feet further up. This means the left feet are blocked from moving up at rows 14-18.
Wait, but earlier I observed that the left feet at rows 14-18 can move up to rows 9-13. Let me check the history.
Actually, earlier I observed:
- After pressing UP from rows 19-23: left feet moved to rows 14-18
- After pressing UP from rows 14-18: left feet moved to rows 9-13 (I think this was in a different context)
Wait, let me check the history more carefully.
Looking at the previous turns:
- Turn 129: Left feet at rows 19-23, cols 14-18. Pressed UP. Result: rows 14-18, cols 14-18.
- Turn 130: Left feet at rows 14-18, cols 14-18. Pressed SPACE. Result: rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (no change).
- Turn 131: Left feet at rows 14-18, cols 14-18. Pressed DOWN. Result: rows 19-23, cols 14-18.
- Turn 132: Left feet at rows 19-23, cols 14-18. Pressed UP, UP. Result: rows 14-18, cols 14-18.
So the left feet at rows 19-23 moved to rows 14-18 after one UP, and then the second UP didn't move them further. This means the left feet are blocked from moving up at rows 14-18.
But earlier, I observed that the left feet at rows 14-18 can move up to rows 9-13. Wait, no, I didn't observe that. I only observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 moved to rows 14-18 after one UP.
Let me check if the left feet at rows 14-18 are blocked from moving up.
Actually, looking at the current state, the left feet are at rows 14-18, cols 14-18. The head is at rows 9-13, cols 24-48. So the left feet are not at the head yet.
Let me try pressing UP again to see if the left feet can move up further.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (right)
# Left feet seem blocked from moving up at rows 14-18. Let me verify by pressing UP again.

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Also check if left feet are blocked by looking at the body shape
print("\nBody shape (black nodes):")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'B' and node['pixels'] > 100:  # Large black nodes are body parts
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Body: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={node['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 44-48, cols 39-43

Body shape (black nodes):
Body: rows 9-58, cols 9-53, pixels=1325
